contents: Systematic review 2017/05/30Aim: The aim of this study was to compare the effects of various immunotherapeutic agents and chemotherapy for unresected or metastatic melanomas. Methods: We performed a network meta-analysis using a Bayesian statistical model to compare objective response rate (ORR) of various immunotherapies from 12 randomized controlled studies. Results: The estimated ORRs of immunotherapy and chemotherapy were 0.224 and 0.108, respectively. The ORRs of immunotherapy in untreated and pretreated patients were 0.279 and 0.176, respectively. In network meta-analysis, the odds ratios for ORR of nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipilmumab (3 mg/kg), pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg and nivolumab 3 mg/kg were 8.54, 5.39 and 4.35, respectively, compared with chemotherapy alone. Conclusion: Our data showed that various immunotherapies had higher ORRs rather than chemotherapy alone.First draft submitted: 13 December 2017; Accepted for publication: 7 March 2017; Published online: 5 May 2017Keywords:  immunotherapy * malignant melanoma * network meta-analysis * objective response rate * traditional meta-analysis

The incidence of melanoma continues to rise during the past 40 years despite most cancer types are stable or declining [1]. Advanced melanoma, including metastatic and unresectable cases, accounts for 4% of all melanoma at diagnosis [2]. The median overall survival is 6.2 months and the 1-year survival rate is 25.5% for stage IV mela-noma patients [3]. The 5-year survival for patients with metastatic melanoma is less than 16% [4]. Melanoma is treated with a combination of surgery, traditional cyto-toxic chemotherapy, targeted therapies and immune-based therapies. Due to progress in the understanding of melanoma biology, breakthroughs in both molecular-targeted therapies and immunotherapies have revolu-tionized the standard of care for patients with advanced melanoma [5]. BRAF mutation that is found in 40-60% of cutaneous mela-nomas is one of the targets of therapeutic strategies [6-8]. Immunotherapy with check-point inhibitors involves the a ugmentation of cell-mediated immunity and anticancer immune response by blocking key check-point m olecules [9,10].To date, the most clinically advanced checkpoint inhibitors are blocking monoclo-nal antibodies that target CTLA-4 and PD-1 pathways. Ipilimumab (antibody against CTLA-4), pembrolizumab and nivolumab (antibodies against PD-1) are the three agents approved by the US FDA for treating advanced melanoma [11]. These immunother-apeutic agents have shown survival gain and definitive objective response rate [ORR] in randomized controlled trials (RCTs) [12-23]. In addition, they have less adverse effects than chemotherapy alone [17,22]. In general, pembrolizumab and nivolumab can be used before ipilimumab. A recent pooled analy-sis of overall survival has provided evidence supporting the durability of long-term sur-vival in a proportion of ipilimumab-treated

Jung-Soo Pyo1 &  Guhyun Kang*,21Department of Pathology, Eulji University Hospital, Eulji University School of Medicine, Daejeon, 35233, Republic of Korea 2Department of Pathology, Inje University Sanggye Paik Hospital, 1342 Dongil-ro, Nowon-gu, Seoul 139-707, Republic of Korea *Author for correspondence:  Tel.: +82 2 950 1260 Fax: +82 2 951 6964 part of

For reprint orders, please contact: patients with advanced melanoma [24]. However, direct comparisons between various immunothera-peutic modalities have not been fully investigated. In addition, the therapeutic effects of immunotherapies in various populations such as patients previously treated by chemotherapy or immunotherapy and BRAF wild-type patients remain unclear. Therefore, the objective of this study was to determine the ORRs of various immunotherapies and chemotherapy alone in advanced melanomas through traditional meta-analysis. Using network meta-analysis, comparisons of ORRs between various immunotherapies and chemo-therapy alone were conducted. In addition, subgroup analysis based on previous treatment was performed to confirm the therapeutic effects of immunotherapy in advanced melanomas.Materials & methodsSearch strategy for published studyRelevant articles were obtained by searching PubMed and MEDLINE databases through 15 June 2016. These databases were searched using the keywords 'melanoma', 'randomized controlled trial (RCT)' and 'immunotherapy, ipilimumab, nivolumab or pembroli-zumab'. The titles and abstracts of all searched articles were screened for exclusion. Review articles were also screened to find additional eligible studies.Selection criteriaArticles were included if the study was performed on human melanomas and if there was information about the ORR of melanoma patients after immunotherapy. All RCTs that compared at least two different treat-ment regimens involving immunotherapy or chemo-therapy were obtained. Articles were excluded if they were RCTs or if they were not written in English.

Data extractionData from all eligible studies were extracted by two independent authors. The included data were extracted from each of the eligible studies [12-23], including the first author's name, year of publication, randomized phase, characteristics and number of enrolled patients, and regimens of immunotherapy and chemotherapy.Data analysesTo perform traditional meta-analysis, data were ana-lyzed using the Comprehensive Meta-Analysis soft-ware package (Biostat, NJ, USA). We investigated the ORR of melanoma patients in various immu-notherapy and chemotherapy groups. The ORR defined as complete response or partial response was according to Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors. Subgroup analysis was performed based on the c ombination of chemotherapy and the presence of previous treatment. Heterogeneity between studies was checked by the Q and R statistics and expressed as p-values. Additionally, sensitivity analysis was con-ducted to assess the heterogeneity of eligible studies and the impact of each study on combined effects. To compare the differences between subgroups, the meta-regression test was performed. In the meta-analysis, because eligible studies used various popu-lations, a random-effect model rather than a fixed-effect model was used. To assess publication bias, Begg's funnel plot and Egger's test were used. Results were considered as statistically significant when the p-value was less than 0.05.For network meta-analysis, the Bayesian consistency

model as described in NetMetaXL was used. To pro-vide an interface for conducting a Bayesian network meta-analysis, WinBUGS 1.4.3 (MRC Biostatistics Unit, Cambridge, UK) was used [25]. We specified the number of iterations and burn-ins as 100,000 for assess-ing convergence of parameter estimates. The evidence of network was first generated. Width of each edge was proportional to the number of RCTs comparing each pair of treatments and the size of each treatment node was proportional to the number of randomized partici-pants. To compare the therapeutic effect, odds ratios (ORs) for ORRs of two treatment modalities were calculated. When the corresponding value of OR was over 1, the therapeutic effect between two therapeutic modalities was better. Values of surface under cumula-tive ranking probability curve (SUCRA) were calcu-lated through the above program. SUCRA would be 1 when a treatment is certain to be the best or 0 when a treatment is certain to be the worst [25].ResultsSelection & characteristics of studiesIn the present study, 105 reports were identified in the database search. Among the searched reports, 36 were excluded due to lack of information or with insufficient information. In addition, 57 reports focused on other diseases (n = 6), nonoriginal articles (n = 34), duplicated articles (n = 10), used animals or cell lines (n = 3) or articles in a language other than English (n = 4) were excluded. Twelve RCTs were ultimately included. A traditional meta-analysis and a network meta-analysis were performed (Figure 1). The characteristics and the evidence of network of eligible studies are shown in Table 1 and Figure 2, respectively [12-23]. Direct compari-sons among ipilimumab alone, nivolumab/ipilimumab and chemotherapy alone were not present (Figure 2A). In addition, in untreated patients, there were no direct comparisons among nivolumab/ipilimumab, pembroli-zumab, ipilmumab and chemotherapy alone (Figure 2B).

105 studies identified throughdatabase searchingPrimary selection throughbrowsing the retrieved titles and abstracts25 full-text articles assessedfor eligibility80 studies excluded:34: non-original article23: no or insufficient information10: duplication6: studies for other disease4: articles of non-English3: non-human13 studies excluded due to noor insufficient information12 studies included in the meta-analysisComparison of ORR from traditional  meta-analysisWe first analyzed the ORRs after various treatments through traditional meta-analysis. The estimated ORRs of immunotherapy and chemotherapy were 0.224 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.169-0.290) and 0.108 (95% CI: 0.071-0.161), respectively. In subgroup treated immunotherapy, the ORRs of immu-notherapy alone and combined immunotherapy and chemotherapy were 0.222 (95% CI: 0.165-0.292) and 0.239 (95% CI: 0.087-0.508), respectively (Table 2). In untreated and pretreated patients, the ORRs of immunotherapy were 0.279 (95% CI: 0.195-0.383) and 0.176 (95% CI: 0.120-0.251), respectively. The ORRs of chemotherapy in untreated and pretreated patients were 0.131 (95% CI: 0.095-0.177) and 0.045 (95% CI: 0.023-0.087), respectively. In sub-group analysis based on previous treatment modality, the ORRs of immunotherapy of patients previously treated with immunotherapy and chemotherapy were 0.239 (95% CI: 0.204-0.277) and 0.051 (95% CI: 0.015-0.159), respectively. In subgroup analysis based on immunotherapeutic agents, the estimated ORR of nivolumab-based immunotherapy was 0.462 (95% CI: 0.370-0.557), higher than that of pembrolizumab- and ipilmumab-based immunotherapies (0.264, 95% CI: 0.212-0.324 and 0.129, 95% CI: 0.089-0.185, respectively).

Network meta-analysisThe ORs for ORR of nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipil-mumab (3 mg/kg), pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg, pem-brolizumab 2 mg/kg and nivolumab 3 mg/kg regi-mens were 8.54 (95% CI: 3.78-21.70), 5.39 (95% CI: 2.52-12.18), 4.64 (95% CI: 2.00-11.65) and 4.35 (95% CI: 2.33-11.79), respectively, compared with chemotherapy alone (Figure 3A). The ORs for ORRs of ipilimumab 10 mg/kg alone, combination of ililmumab (10 mg/kg) and chemotherapy, and ipi-limumab 3 mg/kg alone were 2.93 (95% CI: 0.75-11.79), 2.20 (95% CI: 1.20-4.83) and 1.27 (95% CI: 0.57-2.60), respectively, which were higher than that of chemotherapy alone. However, the ORR of ipili-mumab 0.3 mg/kg alone was lower than that of che-motherapy alone. The values of SUCRA were 0.9626 and 0.7923 for nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipilmumab (3 mg/kg) and pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg, respec-tively. In chemotherapy alone, the value of SUCRA was 0.1599. In rank probability test, combined nivolumab (1 mg/kg) and ipilmumab (3 mg/kg) was the best treatment modality. In untreated patients, the ORs of nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipilmumab (3 mg/kg) compared with nivolumab 3 mg/kg, pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg and chemotherapy alone were 7.47 (95% CI: 1.09-55.54), 1.90 (95% CI: 0.43-9.72) and 1.99 (95% CI: 0.26-20.15), respectively (Figure 3B). The value of SUCRA of nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipilmumab (3 mg/kg) in untreated patients was 0.9200, which was superior to other treatment modalities.DiscussionSelective BRAF inhibitors and combined BRAF/MEK inhibitors are standard treatment for patients with BRAF V600E-mutant advanced melanoma [26]. Recently, various immunotherapeutic agents have been introduced and studied in advanced melanomas. The current study was the first network meta-analysis of published randomized controlled studies to determine the therapeutic effects of various immunotherapy on advanced melanomas.Currently, various treatment modalities such as

chemotherapeutic agents, targeted agent and immu-notherapeutic agents, are applied to advanced mela-nomas. However, direct comparisons between various treatment modalities have not been fully investigated. Dequen et al. have reported a direct comparison for overall survival among anti-CTLA-4 antibody, ipi-lilmumab and traditional chemotherapeutic agent for pretreated unresectable melanomas [2]. However, there was no information for ORR in that network meta-analysis [2]. Mai et al. have reported the results of a network meta-analysis to determine the thera-peutic effect of targeted therapy using a combination of BRAF and MEK inhibitors [8]. Immunotherapy in advanced melanoma    Systematic reviewgeted therapy was found to be higher than that of tra-ditional chemotherapy alone in their network meta-analysis [8]. Comparisons between immunotherapies have only been reported for nivolumab and ipilim-umab in previous RCTs [12-23]. No direct compari-sons have been made between combined nivolumab/ipilimumab and chemotherapy alone, ipilimumab, and pembrolizumab in previous RCTs (Figure 2). The network meta-analysis performed in this study will be useful for interpreting the effects of various immunotherapies or between immunotherapy and chemotherapy.In previous studies, treatments using anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 antibodies showed 30-40% [21,27] and 10-15% of ORR, respectively [19,22]. The comple-mentary and nonredundant mechanisms of PD-1 and CTLA-4 provide a rationale for combination therapy. Earlier animal studies have demonstrated that both inhibitors can act in synergy [28]. Strikingly, Chap-man et al. have recently reported a case of rapid eradica-tion of a large melanoma mass after a single treatment with a combination of nivolumab and ipilimumab [29]. Our current network meta-analysis revealed that com-bined nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipilimumab (3 mg/kg) had the highest ORs and SUCRA value compared with other treatment modalities. Our findings fur-ther support the NCCN guidelines that anti-PD-1 monotherapy or in combination with ipilimumab are the first choices for melanoma patients without BRAF mutation.

Previous studies have shown that the expression of programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1) is associated with high ORRs in patients treated with nivolumab alone [14,22,30-31]. However, the correlation between PD-L1 expression and the therapeutic effect of immu-notherapy has not been fully elucidated. In combina-tion of nivolumab/ipilimumab, the correlation between PD-L1 expression and therapeutic effect is controver-sial [14,16]. In addition, using anti-PD-1 antibody as indi-cation for immunotherapy is unclear, although these treatment modalities have been applied in melanomas with progression after ipilimumab therapy or BRAF mutated melanomas after BRAF inhibition therapy. In our unpublished data, the rate of PD-L1 IHC expression in melanomas was 0.537 (95% CI: 0.349-0.714). There was no significant correlation between PD-L1 expres-sion and worse survival rate. These results suggest that negative expression of PD-L1 should not be exclusion criteria for immunotherapy.In our traditional meta-analysis, the ORRs of immunotherapy and chemotherapy alone in untreated patients were 0.279 (95% CI: 0.195-0.383) and 0.131 (95% CI: 0.095-0.177), respectively. There was no sig-nificant difference between the two groups (p = 0.091). In untreated patients, there have been no direct compar-isons among chemotherapy alone, pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg, nivolumab/ipilimumab and ipilimumab 3 mg/kg. In the current network meta-analysis for untreated patients, the ORs for ORR in nivolumab/ipilimumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg and ipilimumab/CTx were 7.47 (95% CI: 1.09-55.54), 3.92 (95% CI: 0.82-17.78), 3.73 (95% CI: 0.27-41.29) and 2.29 (95% CI: 0.75-8.71), respectively, compared with che-motherapy alone. However, ipilimumab with low dose (3 mg/kg) in untreated patients had lower ORR than Ipilimumab/chemotherapyIpilimumab0.3 mg/kgIpilimumab 3mg/kg

Ipilimumab 10 mg/kgIpilimumab 3 mg/kgNivolumab/ipilimumab Pembrolizumab10 mg/kgChemotherapyNivolumabSystematic review    Pyo & KangPrevious RCTs have investigated the therapeutic effects according to the doses of drug used [12,14,17,23]. In immunotherapy with ipilimumab, patients with high dose showed higher ORR than patients with low dose. The ORRs of high and low doses of pem-brolizumab are controversial. However, in the present network meta-analysis, ORRs of pembrolizumab 10 and 2 mg/kg were higher than that of chemotherapy alone. The OR between pembrolizumab 10 and 2 mg/kg was 1.17 (95% CI: 0.60-2.19). Interestingly, ORR of ipilimumab 0.3 mg/kg was lower than that of che-motherapy alone. However, the ORRs of ipilimumab 10 and 3 mg/kg were significantly higher than that of ipilmumab 0.3 mg/kg.Although, various targeted therapies using BRAF inhibitor have been used in advanced melanomas with BRAF mutation [8,26], other treatment modal-ity for BRAF wild-type melanomas is needed. It has been reported that there is no significant difference in ORR between nivolumab/ipilimumab and ipi-limumab in untreated melanomas patients with and without BRAF mutation [16]. In addition, BRAF mutation does not affect the ORR of nivolumab immunotherapy [22]. When melanomas shows refrac-tory against BRAF inhibitors, recommendation of the second-line therapy is unclear. In the present study, subgroup analysis could not be performed for patients treated with BRAF inhibitors due to limited information of eligible studies. However, according to the overall results, immunotherapy could be useful for treating advanced melanomas regardless of BRAF mutation.The current study has a number of limitations that

should be mentioned. First, in the current study, we performed a subgroup analysis according to p revious therapy. However, some eligible studies were not clearly defined for enrolled patients having previ-ous therapy. In addition, a network meta-analysis for p retreated patients could not be performed due to limited information. Because of this limitation, cumulative studies according to previous therapy sta-tus of patients are needed. Second, the present study could not perform the analysis for the duration of response with treatment. Additional investigation on this will be useful for confirming the effect of i mmunotherapy.ConclusionIn conclusion, our data showed that various immu-notherapies had more effects than chemotherapy  Number of subsetsFixed effect (95% CI)Heterogeneity test (p-value)Random effect (95% CI)Egger's TestImmunotherapy in advanced melanoma    Systematic review

alone for advanced melanomas. Combined treatment with nivolumab and ipilimumab is the most effective immunotherapy modality for advanced melanomas. Cumulative studies with various populations such as patients with previous chemotherapy and those with BRAF mutation will be needed for further analysis.Future perspectiveAs the therapeutic efficacies of newly developed sin-gle agents or combinations of other immunological approaches can be easily compared with this study, the immunotherapy for melanoma can be rapidly inno-vated and applicable.1.58(0.66 -4.13)Pembrolizumab10 mg/kgNivolumab/ipilimumabSystematic review    Pyo & KangFinancial & competing interests disclosureThe authors have no relevant affiliations or financial involve-ment  with  any  organization  or  entity  with  a  financial  inter-est in or financial conflict with the subject matter or mate-rials discussed in the manuscript. In addition, for investigations in-volving human subjects, informed consent has been obtained from the participants involved.Executive summaryBackground*	 The incidence of melanoma continues to rise during the past 40 years despite most cancer types are stable or declining.*	 Advanced melanoma, including metastatic and unresectable cases, accounts for 4% of all melanoma at diagnosis.*	 Melanoma is treated with a combination of surgery, traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy, targeted therapies and immune-based therapies.

*	 To date, the most clinically advanced checkpoint inhibitors are blocking monoclonal antibodies that target CTLA-4 and PD-1 pathways.*	 Ipilimumab (antibody against CTLA-4), pembrolizumab and nivolumab (antibodies against PD-1) are the three agents approved by the US FDA for treating advanced melanoma.Methods*	 A network meta-analysis using a Bayesian statistical model was performed to compare objective response rate (ORR) of various immunotherapies from 12 randomized controlled studies.Results*	 The estimated ORRs of immunotherapy and chemotherapy were 0.224 (95% CI: 0.169-0.290) and 0.108 (95% CI: 0.071-0.161), respectively.*	 The ORs for ORR of nivolumab (1 mg/kg)/ipilmumab (3 mg/kg), pembrolizumab 10 mg/kg, pembrolizumab 2 mg/kg and nivolumab 3 mg/kg regimens were 8.54 (95% CI: 3.78-21.70), 5.39 (95% CI: 2.52-12.18), 4.64 (95% CI: 2.00-11.65) and 4.35 (95% CI: 2.33-11.79), respectively, compared with chemotherapy alone.*	 The ORs for ORRs of ipilimumab 10 mg/kg alone, combination of ililmumab (10 mg/kg) and chemotherapy, and ipilimumab 3 mg/kg alone were 2.93 (95% CI: 0.75-11.79), 2.20 (95% CI: 1.20-4.83) and 1.27 (95% CI: 0.57-2.60), respectively, which were higher than that of chemotherapy alone.*	 The ORR of ipilimumab 0.3 mg/kg alone was lower than that of chemotherapy alone.Discussion & future perspective*	 The current study was the first network meta-analysis of published randomized controlled studies to determine the therapeutic effects of various immunotherapy on advanced melanomas.*	 Various immunotherapies had more effects than chemotherapy alone for advanced melanomas.*	 Combined treatment with nivolumab and ipilimumab is the most effective immunotherapy modality for advanced melanomas.

*	 With a comparison of this network meta-analysis, the newly developed immunotherapeutic agents will be rapidly innovated and applicable.References1 Siegel R, Ma J, Zou Z, Jemal A.
